title: Realm Roles vs Client Roles
description:
## Scoping a Role to One App

You run several applications in a single realm. You need to define a role called `report-editor` that is **only meaningful inside one specific application** — the *Billing* app — and should not pollute the global role list shared by every client.

How should you define `report-editor`?

options.text: As a **client role** on the Billing client — client roles are namespaced to that one client and only appear under `resource_access.billing.roles` in tokens
As a **realm role** — realm roles are the only kind that can be assigned to users
As a **group** named `report-editor`, since groups replace roles in Keycloak
As a **realm role** with a name prefixed by the client, e.g. `billing_report-editor`, since client roles cannot be assigned to users

explanation: **Realm roles** are global to the realm and shared across every client, while **client roles** are *namespaced* to a single client — perfect for a role that only means something inside one application. Defining `report-editor` as a client role on the Billing client keeps it out of the global realm-role list and surfaces it in tokens under `resource_access.billing.roles`. (a) is correct. (b) is wrong — *both* realm and client roles can be assigned to users. (c) is wrong — groups are a way to *bundle and assign* roles, not a replacement for them. (d) works mechanically but defeats the purpose: it still creates a global realm role and just fakes namespacing with a naming convention, which is exactly what client roles exist to avoid.

hints.title: Where does it show up in the token?
hints.body: Realm roles land in `realm_access.roles`; client roles land in `resource_access.<clientId>.roles`. The token layout itself tells you which scope a role belongs to.
